did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a deep learning pipeline consisting of a frozen ResNet50 backbone for feature extraction and a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) model for classification to diagnose basal cell carcinoma (BCC) using reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is classified as a 'Brief Report' and describes an original retrospective observational study evaluating the diagnostic performance of a BiLSTM deep learning model on RCM images. It presents new experimental results, including internal cross-validation (mean AUC 0.984) and external validation on independent cohorts (MSKCC and Hames), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning Analysis of Reflectance Confocal Microscopy for Basal Cell Carcinoma Diagnosis
brief_summary: This retrospective study evaluated a deep learning pipeline using a frozen ResNet50 backbone and a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) model to diagnose basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from in vivo reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) images. The internal dataset comprised 230 histopathologically confirmed lesions from the University of Modena and Reggio Emilia, including BCC and various non-BCC controls. Model performance was assessed via five-fold stratified cross-validation, achieving a mean AUC of 0.984, with an overall accuracy of 93.5%. External validation was conducted on two independent cohorts, the MSKCC and Hames datasets, yielding AUCs of 0.735 and 0.864, respectively. The results demonstrate that the BiLSTM-based approach can achieve high diagnostic accuracy for BCC, although performance decreased in external validation due to domain shift. The study highlights the feasibility of multi-institutional external validation for RCM-based AI diagnostics without manual annotation.

all_task_types.target: Diagnosis of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. non-BCC lesions
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing BCC from heterogeneous non-BCC controls (including melanocytic nevi, melanomas, seborrheic keratoses, actinic keratosis, lichen planus-like keratosis, and desmoplastic trichoepithelioma) using a BiLSTM model on RCM images. Performance evaluated via AUC, sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.

datasets_used.name: University of Modena and Reggio Emilia (Internal Cohort)
datasets_used.description: Retrospective observational dataset collected at the University of Modena and Reggio Emilia, Italy, containing clinical data and in-vivo RCM images acquired with a Vivastack-3000 device. All lesions were histopathologically confirmed.

use.training: Used for model training and validation via five-fold stratified cross-validation with lesion-level grouping.
use.validation: Used for model training and validation via five-fold stratified cross-validation with lesion-level grouping.
use.testing: Used for model training and validation via five-fold stratified cross-validation with lesion-level grouping.

datasets_used.name: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC) RCM Dataset
datasets_used.description: Publicly available independent external validation cohort containing RCM images.

use.testing: Used for external validation of the diagnostic model.

datasets_used.name: Hames RCM Dataset
datasets_used.description: Publicly available independent external validation cohort consisting of RCM images from healthy volunteers.

use.testing: Used for external validation of the diagnostic model (combined with MSKCC for combined external validation).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Model training and validation with lesion-level grouping to ensure no data leakage between folds.
To report the central tendency and variability of diagnostic performance across the five cross-validation folds (mean AUC 0.984±0.012).
To report overall diagnostic performance metrics across the validation sets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Diagnostic performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics, including mean Area Under the Curve (AUC) with standard deviation, sensitivity, specificity, and overall accuracy.

University of Modena and Reggio Emilia (.notes: Model training and validation used lesion-level grouping to ensure no data leakage. The dataset comprised 230 histopathologically confirmed lesions (82 BCC, 148 non-BCC).

MSKCC RCM Dataset.notes: Independent external validation cohort containing 61 lesions (39 BCC, 22 non-BCC).

Combined External Validation (MSKCC and.notes: Combined validation of MSKCC (n=61) and Hames (n=54 healthy volunteers) cohorts, total n=115. The reduction in AUC from 0.984 to 0.864 reflects domain shift.

architecture_used.main_model: Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) with a frozen ResNet50 backbone
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet50 backbone was frozen (implies use of pre-trained weights, likely ImageNet, though not explicitly stated as 'ImageNet' in the text, 'frozen' indicates pre-training was utilized and weights were not updated during this study's training phase).
architecture_used.input_size: Individual RCM frames (specific dimensions not reported; processed to extract 2048-dimensional embeddings)

architecture_details.sequence_processing: Embeddings from individual RCM frames were concatenated as ordered sequences

training_details.validation_strategy: Five-fold stratified cross-validation with lesion-level grouping
training_details.data_leakage_prevention: All frames from a given lesion were confined to a single fold

classification_or_output_head.output_metric: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)

preprocessing_techniques: Individual RCM frames were processed through a frozen ResNet50 backbone to extract 2048-dimensional embeddings
Embeddings were concatenated as ordered sequences
Lesion-level grouping was used to ensure all frames from a given lesion were confined to a single fold (data leakage prevention)
conclusions: A BiLSTM-based deep learning model applied to in vivo RCM images achieved high diagnostic accuracy in distinguishing basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from other cutaneous lesions, with an internal mean AUC of 0.984±0.012, sensitivity of 90.2%, specificity of 95.3%, and overall accuracy of 93.5%.
The study demonstrated the feasibility of multi-institutional external validation using two publicly available independent cohorts (MSKCC and Hames), yielding a combined external AUC of 0.864.
The reduction in AUC from 0.984 (internal) to 0.864 (external) reflects domain shift and the broad diagnostic spectrum of the external cohort.
Unlike prior approaches, this study required no manual annotation and included histopathology confirmation for all internal training and test cases.
The results are specific to the datasets used, with cases stratified by lesion rather than patient, and the BCC prevalence is not reflective of the general population.
The study is limited by its retrospective design and the inclusion of limited clinically challenging mimickers of BCC.
Further prospective studies incorporating additional interpretability analysis and clinically challenging mimickers of BCC are warranted.
important_remarks: The study is limited by its retrospective design and the inclusion of limited clinically challenging mimickers of BCC.
Results are specific to the datasets used; cases were stratified by lesion rather than patient, and the BCC prevalence is not reflective of the general population.
Performance decreased in external validation (AUC 0.864) compared to internal validation (AUC 0.984), reflecting domain shift and the broad diagnostic spectrum of the external cohort.
The study addresses the scarce literature regarding the utility of deep learning for BCC diagnosis via RCM and demonstrates feasibility with multi-institutional external validation.
Further prospective studies incorporating additional interpretability analysis and clinically challenging mimickers of BCC are warranted.
